elements.sent-2411-054: We start by reviewing the FL conjecture in general dimension and take this opportunity to introduce the notation we will be using.
elements.sent-2411-055: One argues for the FL bound by studying the evaporation of large charged black holes in dS space.
elements.sent-2411-056: These are solutions to Einstein-Maxwell theory in dS space called Nariai black holes and they can shed their mass by the usual Hawking process, emitting neutral particles such as gravitons or photons.
elements.sent-2411-057: In addition, they can shed their charge by a Schwinger process which screens the electric field.
elements.sent-2411-058: In the presence of light charged matter, Schwinger pair production can become very fast and leads to the electric field being screened on time scales much shorter than the Hawking radiation time scale.
elements.sent-2411-059: In particular, the Schwinger rate is set by the magnitude of the electric field which is comparable to $M_d^{\frac{d {2 -1 H$ where $M_d$ is the $d$-dimensional Planck constant and $H$ is the Hubble rate, as we will see below.}}}

elements.sent-2411-388: The branes studied in the previous section were inspired by the D-branes present in string theory.
elements.sent-2411-389: These objects feature world-volume degrees of freedom originating from open strings ending on these branes.
elements.sent-2411-390: These degrees of freedom include world-volume gauge fields that played an important role in our analysis.
elements.sent-2411-391: In particular, the world-volume gauge fields acquired a non-trivial classical profile in the solutions that we studied and this allowed the brane to couple to the bulk gauge fields via the Wess-Zumino terms in Eq.~\eqref{eq:evenpAction and Eq.~\eqref{eq:oddpAction .}}
elements.sent-2411-392: Due to this coupling and the non-trivial gauge field profile, the branes obtain an electric charge and are able to screen the bulk electric fields.
elements.sent-2411-393: More generally, we may imagine extended sources without such world-volume gauge fields.
elements.sent-2411-394: These could represent solitonic objects in quantum field theory or unknown objects yet to be discovered in string theory.
elements.sent-2411-395: An axion domain wall provides an example of these objects (and we will say more about them below) since it couples to bulk 1-form gauge fields without having world-volume-localised gauge fields.
elements.sent-2411-396: In this section, we will study branes of this type which are inspired by axion domain walls.
elements.sent-2411-397: The aim is to derive FL bounds on the properties of 2-dimensional branes without world-volume gauge fields.
elements.sent-2411-398: The general setup we study is the same as the one we considered in section~\ref{sec:FLforBranes .}
elements.sent-2411-399: Namely, we will consider electrodynamics coupled to gravity in de Sitter space, Eq.~\eqref{eq:EinsteinMaxwell .}
elements.sent-2411-400: In addition, we will assume the theory also contains 2+1 dimensional branes with worldvolume action given by: \begin{equation}\label{eq:braneWV} I_{\rm 2-brane} = \int_{WV} \Bigg( T_2 *1 - \frac{g^2}{4\pi} A \wedge F \Bigg),\end{equation} where $T_2$ is the tension of the 2-brane, $g$ is gauge coupling of the theory of electrodynamics.
elements.sent-2411-401: As mentioned above, this coupling to bulk gauge fields resembles the axion domain wall coupling.
elements.sent-2411-402: The axion domain wall is the special case where the tension $T_2$ is also determined by the axion mass and decay constant and is given by $T_2\sim mf^2$.
elements.sent-2411-403: Nonetheless, the bound we derive in this section will not apply to axion domain walls as we explain below.
elements.sent-2411-404: Branes with similar couplings may also be constructed within string theory in the context of the quantum Hall effect~\cite{Bergman:2004mv .}
elements.sent-2411-405: In previous sections, we studied electrically charged Nariai solutions of the theory in Eq.~\eqref{eq:EinsteinMaxwell .}
elements.sent-2411-406: This theory also has dyonic Nariai black hole solutions~\cite{Lee:1991jw,Balakin:2017nbg which will be the focus of this section.}
elements.sent-2411-407: Analogous to their electrically charged counterparts, these Nariai solutions are $dS_2 \times S^2$ spacetimes with constant parallel electric and magnetic fields stretching between the black hole and de Sitter horizons.

elements.sent-2411-409: In a previous paper~\cite{Hassan:2024nbl , we showed that such a (flat space) field configuration is unstable to nucleating axion domain walls.}

elements.subsection-2411-010: The FL bound in Type II
elements.sent-2411-532: With the above preliminaries out of the way, we are now ready to identify the coefficients $g$ that feature in our discussion of the FL bound in section \ref{sec:FLforBranes .}

elements.sent-2411-548: Let us demonstrate the above claim in the simple example of a D2-brane.

elements.sent-2411-695: Finally, for branes with world-volume gauge fields, we verified our inequalities using the properties of D-brane in Type II string theory and found that they are satisfied.
elements.sent-2411-696: Several promising directions remain open to further explore the FL bound.
elements.sent-2411-697: First, there are the unsettled topics discussed in section~\ref{sec:FLReview which also pertain to the original FL proposal.}

elements.sent-2411-700: It is important to carry out a study that includes all decay channels and the effects of particle--anti-particle annihilations.
elements.sent-2411-701: These detailed studies may also determine the prefactor in the FL bounds.

elements.sent-2411-703: The same prefactor was found in the calculations of~\cite{Aalsma:2023mkz .}
elements.sent-2411-704: An interesting task is to check whether this prefactor is affected by the inclusion of additional decay channels.
elements.sent-2411-705: Additionally, one can look for connections between the original FL proposal~\cite{Montero:2019ekk and the new inequalities presented in this paper.}

elements.sent-2411-709: This work obtained a bound on the axion decay constant using the geometry of the internal manifold.
elements.sent-2411-710: Notably, this does not constrain the axion mass which raises the intriguing possibility of seeking such a constraint on axion masses.

elements.sent-2411-713: In section~\ref{sec:stringtheory , we checked our bounds against the properties of D-branes in string theory and found that they are all satisfied in the parametrically weak coupling regime.}
elements.sent-2411-714: We note that there is a more general version of the calculations we performed, which includes all couplings (such as $\gamma$ and $g_{(k)}$) from the beginning, that will remain valid without having to take parametrically small string coupling.
elements.sent-2411-715: We leave this exercise, which produces more general and complicated bounds, for future work.
elements.sent-2411-716: The advantage of these more general bounds is that they can be compared directly to string theory branes without the need to assume arbitrarily small $g_s$ and are therefore more relevant for dS constructions which may not exist at parametrically weak coupling.
elements.sent-2411-717: We end this section by commenting on potential phenomenological applications of the bounds we derived in this work.
elements.sent-2411-718: As pointed out in~\cite{Montero:2021otb , the FL bound suggests that dS constructions may be more complicated that previously thought.}
elements.sent-2411-719: In particular, in the context of uplifts using anti-branes in a warped throat, the assumption that the warped throat is decoupled from bulk dynamics may lead to contradictions with the FL bound.
elements.sent-2411-720: For example, FL would forbid light charged matter arising from the bulk manifold away from the warped throat.
elements.sent-2411-721: Similar statements can be made about branes, although, as we saw in section~\ref{sec:stringtheory , it is typically easier for wrapped branes to satisfy our bounds compared to unwrapped branes.}
elements.sent-2411-722: Nonetheless, manifolds may be complicated and there could be scenarios that lead to violations of FL such as the one we describe now.
elements.sent-2411-723: For a wrapped brane, the coupling we rely on in Eq.~\eqref{eq:evenpAction , say, may come from the dimensional reduction of a higher form RR gauge field along the cycle that the brane wraps.}
elements.sent-2411-724: The coefficient of the kinetic term of a 1-form gauge field obtained through this dimensional reduction procedure depends on the particular cycle the brane wraps.
elements.sent-2411-725: Canonical normalization of this kinetic term means that this coefficient affects the coupling that appears in Eq.~\eqref{eq:resultSummary1 .}
elements.sent-2411-726: Consider as an example the case of 2-branes in the non-compact dimensions where the FL bound we present is a lower bound on the brane tension.
elements.sent-2411-727: It is easy to see that the coupling becomes smaller if the 2-brane is obtained by wraping a higher dimension brane on a cycle that is the smallest representative in its homology class.
elements.sent-2411-728: If the brane instead wraps a meta-stable cycle that is not the smallest representative, the coupling may become smaller or larger and may thus violate the inequality we have in Eq.~\eqref{eq:resultSummary1 .}
elements.sent-2411-729: This would be an interesting, although potentially difficult, check to carry out.
elements.sent-2411-730: One could also envision applications of our bounds to early-universe physics.
elements.sent-2411-731: For instance, if we were to determine the Hubble rate during an epoch of inflation, we would obtain lower limits on the tension of 2-branes.
elements.sent-2411-732: Assuming we live in a Type II string theory, these bounds can be translated to bounds on a combination of the string scaled and the string coupling (that describe the tension of D2-branes) and this may explain why string theory was not found at the LHC for example! With the above considerations in mind, we end by highlighting the richness of the FL bound as a topic of exploration from theoretical and phenomenological perspectives.

elements.sent-2411-806: Let us now study the Nariai solutions of these equations.
elements.sent-2411-807: As in the case of the electrically charged Nariai black hole, we expect our dyonic black hole to have spatially constant electromagnetic fields.
elements.sent-2411-808: In our case, this will also imply a spatially constant axion field as parallel electric and magnetic fields act as classical sources of the axion.
elements.sent-2411-809: In our coordinates, the electromagnetic fields $E$ and $B$ are described by the field strength tensor: \begin{equation}\label{eq:EMfields} F_{\mu\nu} =  0 -\frac{a}{\sqrt{\Lambda/3}\tilde{r}}E 0 0 \frac{a}{\sqrt{\Lambda/3}\tilde{r}}E 0 0 0 0 0 0 B \tilde{r}^2 \sin \sigma 0 0 -B \tilde{r}^2 \sin \sigma 0 \end{equation} where $\sigma$ is the polar angle (since we are reserving the symbol $\theta$ for the dimensionless axion field).
elements.sent-2411-810: The value of the (constant) axion field is obtained by solving: \begin{equation}\label{eq:thetavalue} \sin \theta_* = \frac{\alpha}{\pi} \frac{E B}{m^2 f^2}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-811: Note in particular that solutions will have $\alpha E B \leq \pi m^2 f^2$ since $\sin \theta_* \leq 1$.
elements.sent-2411-812: In order to see how this comes about, it is useful to define the black hole charges (rescaled for convenience): \begin{equation}\label{eq:charges} \tilde{Q}_M = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int F \tilde{Q}_E = \frac{1}{4\pi} \int *F + K \frac{\alpha \theta}{\pi} F Q_{M,E} = \sqrt{\frac{\Lambda}{6\Mpl^2}} \tilde{Q}_{M,E}.\end{equation}.
elements.sent-2411-813: In terms of these charges, we have expressions for the electric and magnetic fields (see also \cite{Campbell:1991rz, Balakin:2017nbg ): \begin{equation}B = \frac{1}{\tilde{r}^2}\tilde{Q}_M E = \frac{1}{\tilde{r}^2}\left(\tilde{Q}_E - \frac{\alpha \theta_*}{\pi} \tilde{Q}_M\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-814: Fixing these charges, it is then easy to see that~\eqref{eq:thetavalue always has a solution.}
elements.sent-2411-815: This is because we can always choose large values of $\theta_*$ so that the electric field is small enough which ensures that the right hand side of~\eqref{eq:thetavalue is smaller than unity.}
elements.sent-2411-816: On the other hand, it is not always possible to find real solutions to equation~\eqref{eq:eom1 with constant $S^2$ radius.}
elements.sent-2411-817: Plugging in the expression for the energy density, we find the radius: \begin{equation}\label{eq:S2radiuswithAxion} r^2 = \frac{1+\sqrt{1-12[Q_M^2 + (Q_E- \frac{\alpha}{\pi}\theta Q_M)^2][1 + X(1-\cos \theta)]}}{6[1 + X(1-\cos \theta)]}\end{equation} where we have defined the dimensionless ratio $X \equiv m^2 f^2 / \Mpl^2 \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-818: Demanding that the square root is real implies a bound on the magnetic charge $|Q_M| < 1/\sqrt{12}$.
elements.sent-2411-819: When $Q_M = 0$, there is also a similar bound on the electric charge which is however relaxed with $Q_M \neq 0$ as we can choose a large axion value to reduce the electric field as above.
elements.sent-2411-820: Given the radius of the sphere, we can solve for the axion value $\theta_*$ by subtituting the expressions for the electric and magnetic fields into equation~\eqref{eq:thetavalue .. This gives: \begin{equation}\sin \theta_* = \frac{2\alpha}{3\pi} \frac{\Mpl^2 \Lambda}{m^2 f^2}\frac{1}{r^4} Q_M \left(Q_E - \frac{\alpha \theta_*}{\pi} Q_M\right).\end{equation}.}
elements.sent-2411-821: As we mentioned above, this equation will always have a solution because $\theta_*$ can be chosen so the right hand side is smaller than unity.
elements.sent-2411-822: In particular, as $m\rightarrow 0$, the right hand side would become large unless the expression in parentheses becomes proportional to $X = m^2 f^2/\alpha \Mpl^2 \Lambda$.
elements.sent-2411-823: In this small $m$ limit, we find that the electric and magnetic fields are: \begin{equation}E \sim \Mpl \sqrt{\Lambda} \left(\frac{m f}{g\Mpl H}\right)^2 \label{eq:Efield} B \sim \Mpl \sqrt{\Lambda} \label{eq:Bfield}\end{equation} so that the electric field is parametrically smaller than $\Mpl \sqrt{\Lambda}$.. \bibliographystyle{JHEP.}
